examples.title: User Creation and Privilege Granting
examples.description: Demonstrates how to create database users and assign appropriate privileges for secure access management
examples.explanation: This SQL script demonstrates user management and privilege assignment. The DBA creates users, grants both system privileges (affecting database operation) and object privileges (affecting specific data), uses roles for simplified management, and can revoke or drop users as needed.

examples.title: Database Backup Manager Class
examples.description: Python class demonstrating backup strategy implementation with full, incremental, and restore capabilities
examples.code:
import os
import shutil
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path

class DatabaseBackupManager:
    def __init__(self, backup_dir, db_name):
        self.backup_dir = Path(backup_dir)
        self.db_name = db_name
        self.backup_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        self.last_full_backup = None
        self.last_backup_time = None
    
    def create_full_backup(self):
        """Create a full database backup"""
        timestamp = datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")
        backup_name = f"{self.db_name}_full_{timestamp}.bak"
        backup_path = self.backup_dir / backup_name
        # Simulated backup operation
        print(f"Creating full backup: {backup_name}")
        print(f"Backup location: {backup_path}")
        self.last_full_backup = backup_path
        self.last_backup_time = datetime.now()
        return backup_path
    
    def create_incremental_backup(self):
        """Create incremental backup since last backup"""
        if not self.last_full_backup:
            return self.create_full_backup()
        timestamp = datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")
        backup_name = f"{self.db_name}_incr_{timestamp}.bak"
        backup_path = self.backup_dir / backup_name
        print(f"Creating incremental backup: {backup_name}")
        print(f"Changes since: {self.last_backup_time}")
        return backup_path
    
    def restore_backup(self, backup_file):
        """Restore database from backup"""
        backup_path = self.backup_dir / backup_file
        if not backup_path.exists():
            raise FileNotFoundError(f"Backup file not found: {backup_file}")
        print(f"Restoring from: {backup_file}")
        print("Step 1: Stop database services")
        print("Step 2: Restore data files")
        print("Step 3: Apply transaction logs")
        print("Step 4: Restart database services")
        print("Restore completed successfully!")

# Usage Example
if __name__ == "__main__":
    backup_mgr = DatabaseBackupManager("/backups", "production_db")
    backup_mgr.create_full_backup()
    backup_mgr.create_incremental_backup()
    backup_mgr.restore_backup("production_db_full_20240101_120000.bak")
examples.explanation: This Python class implements a backup management system. It supports full backups (complete database copy) and incremental backups (only changes since last backup). The restore method demonstrates the recovery process steps. In production, this would connect to actual database systems using libraries like psycopg2 or pyodbc.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is database size for full backup

examples.title: Database Recovery Procedures
examples.description: SQL scripts demonstrating various recovery scenarios and techniques
examples.code:
-- Checkpoint: Force dirty pages to disk
CHECKPOINT;

-- Simple recovery: Restore to last backup
RESTORE DATABASE production_db 
FROM DISK = 'C:\backups\full_backup.bak'
WITH REPLACE, RECOVERY;

-- Point-in-time recovery using transaction logs
RESTORE DATABASE production_db 
FROM DISK = 'C:\backups\full_backup.bak'
WITH NORECOVERY;

RESTORE LOG production_db 
FROM DISK = 'C:\backups\log_backup_1.trn'
WITH NORECOVERY;

RESTORE LOG production_db 
FROM DISK = 'C:\backups\log_backup_2.trn'
WITH STOPAT = '2024-01-15 14:30:00', RECOVERY;

-- Verify database integrity after recovery
DBCC CHECKDB ('production_db');

-- Emergency recovery with minimal data loss
RESTORE DATABASE production_db 
FROM DISK = 'C:\backups\full_backup.bak'
WITH REPLACE, RECOVERY;

-- View backup history
SELECT 
    backup_set_id,
    name,
    backup_start_date,
    backup_finish_date,
    type,
    backup_size
FROM msdb.dbo.backupset 
WHERE database_name = 'production_db'
ORDER BY backup_start_date DESC;
examples.explanation: These SQL commands demonstrate database recovery operations. CHECKPOINT forces changes to disk. RESTORE DATABASE recovers from full backups. Point-in-time recovery uses transaction logs to restore to a specific moment. DBCC CHECKDB verifies data integrity after recovery. The final query shows backup history for auditing purposes.

examples.title: Database Performance Monitoring Queries
examples.description: SQL queries for monitoring database performance, identifying bottlenecks, and optimization opportunities
examples.code:
-- Check active sessions and their status
SELECT 
    spid, 
    login_time, 
    status, 
    hostname, 
    program_name, 
    loginame
FROM sys.sysprocesses
WHERE status = 'runnable';

-- Find expensive queries (by CPU time)
SELECT TOP 10 
    qs.execution_count,
    qs.total_worker_time / 1000 AS total_cpu_ms,
    qs.total_elapsed_time / 1000 AS total_elapsed_ms,
    SUBSTRING(qt.text, 1, 200) AS query_text
FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats qs
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(qs.sql_handle) qt
ORDER BY qs.total_worker_time DESC;

-- Check index usage and recommendations
SELECT 
    OBJECT_NAME(s.object_id) AS table_name,
    i.name AS index_name,
    s.user_seeks,
    s.user_scans,
    s.user_lookups,
    s.user_updates
FROM sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats s
INNER JOIN sys.indexes i ON s.object_id = i.object_id AND s.index_id = i.index_id
WHERE OBJECTPROPERTY(s.object_id, 'IsUserTable') = 1
ORDER BY s.user_seeks + s.user_scans DESC;

-- Monitor lock contention
SELECT 
    l.request_session_id,
    DB_NAME(l.resource_database_id) AS database_name,
    l.request_mode,
    l.request_status,
    t.text
FROM sys.dm_tran_locks l
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(t.sql_handle) t
WHERE l.resource_database_id = DB_ID();

-- Buffer pool memory usage
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) * 8 / 1024 AS buffer_cache_mb,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions) AS active_sessions
FROM sys.dm_os_buffer_descriptors;
examples.explanation: These queries help DBAs monitor database health. sysprocesses shows active connections. dm_exec_query_stats identifies slow queries. dm_db_index_usage_stats shows which indexes are used. dm_tran_locks detects lock contention. dm_os_buffer_descriptors shows memory usage. Regular monitoring helps proactively identify performance issues.
